metadata.title: All Lives Matter discussions on Twitter: Varied use, prevalence, and interpretive repertoires
metadata.journal: Journal of Community & Applied Social Psychology

abstract: All Lives Matter (ALM) has emerged as a response to, and critique of, the Black Lives Matter (BLM) anti-racist movement. ALM has been shown to work to undermine and attempt to deracialise BLM; however, there is a need for a comprehensive understanding of how ALM functions in online interactions. The research questions are therefore: What different ways is ALM used in Twitter debates?, How prevalent are these different uses?, and What function do the different uses of ALM perform in the wider debate around BLM? To address these questions, we employed a mixed-method approach drawing on topic modelling and critical discursive psychology of Twitter posts using the hashtag #AllLivesMatter. A corpus of 294,217 unique tweets sent by 145,994 unique users was subject to Structural Topic Modelling (STM), which resulted in 60 topics, and from this, a sub-dataset of 180 tweets was generated for discourse analysis. The STM identified 12 distinct uses of ALM, ranging from direct and even extreme opposition to BLM to criticisms of ALM and support of BLM and antiracism messages, both of which are explored in the discourse analysis. Together, the analysis suggests that, at least on Twitter, the ALM hashtag is not one-dimensional nor a settled debate. Moreover, the Twitter public can use the ALM hashtag to denounce racism and the ideological pretext of ALM.

sections.paper_id: Goodman et al. - 2024 - All Lives Matter discussions on Twitter Varied use, prevalence, and interpretive repertoires
paragraphs: All Lives Matter (ALM), Black Lives Matter (BLM), discursive psychology, online interaction, racism, structural topic modelling (STM) 'Black Lives Matter' (BLM) first came to prominence in 2014 with the protests that followed the fatal shooting of an unarmed Black teenager, Michael Brown, by a policeman in Missouri, USA. Since then, the phrase has come to be part of a global anti-racism movement, particularly after the murder of George Floyd, another unarmed Black man in the USA. As well as large physical BLM protests across the USA and the world, the phrase BLM has also become prolific in online settings, for example, with above 40 million uses of the phrase on Twitter in the year from June 2014 to May 2015 (Freelon et al., 2016) and more recently, 49.6 million corpus tweets, that made direct reference to BLM (van der Veen, 2022). BLM, 'the civil rights issue of our time' (Holt & Sweitzer, 2020, p. 16), has therefore brought racism and anti-racism clearly into public debate, with one feature of this being the use of alternative slogans and counternarratives in the form of All Lives Matter (ALM), Blue Lives Matter/Back the Blue, and White Lives Matter. ALM, in particular, emerged on social media in 2014 as a reactionary rhetorical slogan and hashtag to BLM evolving out of the 'Blue Lives Matter' countermovement in the United States. While ALM is not a social movement per se, many of its supporters negate the significance of race as it pertains to lives mattering and instead affirm that attention should be given to all lives, regardless of colour.

Previous research has chronicled the debate over the meaning and motivation of All Lives Matter, particularly concerning whether it is a more inclusive slogan or one that opposes the anti-racism message of BLM (Atkins, 2019;Carney, 2016;Gallagher et al., 2018;Kil, 2020;Paul, 2019;West et al., 2021). Atkins (2019) argues that this hinges on different possible interpretations of BLM, where it can mean either 'Black Lives Matter too' or 'only Black Lives Matter' (2019, p. 1, emphasis in original) with the 'too' version being clearly about Black rights and the 'only' version being an example of anti-White racism. Atkins (2019) argues that this supposedly racist 'only' interpretation is mainly made by White people and that this is a misunderstanding that mirrors the way that 'Black Power' was also misinterpreted as being hostile, rather than about equal rights and Black racial pride. West et al.'s (2021) recent analysis was the first to directly evaluate the degree to which racial bias explained support for ALM over BLM. White British participants living in the UK completed measures of preference for ALM vs. BLM, which included questions like 'Which of these two statements (BLM or ALM) would you be more comfortable saying in public', as well as measures of implicit and explicit racism. It was found that implicit racism and colourblind ideology were associated with a preference for ALM over BLM. Other work within the United States has similarly concluded that being White and holding symbolically racist attitudes (Ilchi & Frank, 2021) within a convenience sample, and being older, Republican/conservative, and male among a nationally representative sample (Updegrove et al., 2020), were the strongest predictors of BLM opposition.

In interpreting these findings, West et al. (2021) and others (Paul, 2019) suggest that there is a commitment to ideologies that obfuscate the impact of race and racism in contemporary society and that this commitment explains opposition to BLM. To make race and racism insignificant via colour-blind logics, in particular, has been a foremost framework to contextualise the persistence of racialised dominance and White supremacy (Mills, 2007;Mueller, 2017;Windel et al., 2023). In Racism Without Racists, for example, Bonilla-Silva (2006/2021) attributes the contemporary ideology of colour-blindness as aiding in, 'the maintenance of White privilege without fanfare, without naming those who it subjects and those who it rewards' (pp. 3-4) via frames of abstract liberalism, naturalisation, cultural racism, and minimisation to build 'non-racist' rationales (Bonilla-Silva, 2006/2021). Mueller (2017) further grounds colour-blindness as a 'cultural recursive accomplishment ' (p. 220) and an attachment to a 'process of knowing designed to produce not knowing surrounding White privilege, culpability, and structural White supremacy' (p. 220).
In this regard, if one invests in colour-blindness, then BLM may be heard as exclusionary, and ALM is the suitable response because it conforms to a post-racism world.

Similarly, there is a growing literature looking at the rhetorical use of ALM over BLM, with studies showing (like Atkins, 2019) that the use of ALM undermines the message of BLM. Kil (2020) showed that ALM worked to undermine BLM: by treating ALM as the same as BLM, until BLM came to be presented as less worthy and by equating ALM with White power (and also 'White Lives Matter'). Kil describes ALM as using a 'race-neutral disguise', (2020, p. 30), which again shows how a supposedly colour-blind approach can work against Black rights. D'Ambrosio (2019), Paul (2019) and Carney (2016) further support the idea that the use of ALM works as a colour-blind, deracialising strategy. Augoustinos and Every (2007) show how discursive deracialisation can be an effective rhetorical strategy for presenting racist ideas as non-racist and for furthering exclusionary practises, and it appears that ALM is working in precisely this way in response to BLM. Goodman et al. (2023) analysed Twitter interactions that contained alternative 'lives matter' slogans (anything other than Black Lives Matter) and showed that a variety of different slogans were used, ranging from joke-based versions (such as Benefit/Geordie lives matter) to the more overtly controversial 'White Lives Matter,' but with the most prominent being All Lives Matter. They showed that there were different uses of ALM and categorised these as being supposedly more inclusive than BLM (which reflects the versions depicted by Atkins, 2019;Carney, 2016 among others) as well as signalling more direct opposition to BLM, although they concluded that all versions of ALM, including the supposedly more inclusive type work to undermine and challenge BLM. This demonstrates that rather than one homogenous use of ALM, it can be used in different ways to accomplish different ends. It is therefore necessary to investigate further the differing ways in which the ALM slogan can be used and to what ends.

In sum, the above review opens up questions about language and demonstrates the need for a more comprehensive understanding of the salient topics and different uses of ALM. The research questions being addressed in this paper are therefore: What different ways is ALM used in Twitter debates?, How prevalent are these different uses?, and What function do the different uses of ALM perform in the wider debate around BLM?

sections.paper_id: Goodman et al. - 2024 - All Lives Matter discussions on Twitter Varied use, prevalence, and interpretive repertoires
paragraphs: This paper takes a mixed-method approach drawing on topic modelling and critical discursive psychology (Wetherell, 1998) of Twitter posts of the hashtag #AllLivesMatter. We used Structural Topic Modelling (STM) to identify broad topic patterns of ALM tweets. We conducted a critical discursive psychological analysis to examine the patterns and routines of language use (e.g., interpretive repertoires) of ALM. By integrating quantitative and qualitative analysis, we hope our findings will enable a more nuanced insight into public discourses of ALM, as it unfolds on Twitter.

sections.paper_id: Goodman et al. - 2024 - All Lives Matter discussions on Twitter Varied use, prevalence, and interpretive repertoires
paragraphs: This data was scraped from the Twitter social media and online news platform in September 2022. We used the Twitter Developer Application Protocol Interface (API) to collect English language tweets from Twitter. To connect with the Twitter API, we ran the Rtweet package (Kearney, 2019) in the R statistical software (R Core Team, 2014). Based on our interest in understanding the uses of #AllLivesMatter in the immediate period preceding and in the aftermath of the 2020 summer of Black Lives Matter protests, we decided to scrape tweets for a one-year period starting March 13, 2020, the date of the murder of Breonna Taylor, and ending March 13, 2021. This initial scraping resulted in 320,273 unique tweets, which underwent a process of cleaning. This included removing tweets in languages other than English, omitting hyperlinks, and ensuring the protection of users' anonymity by removing usernames. The final dataset consisted of 294,217 unique tweets sent by 145,994 unique users. We used this final dataset to compute latent themes using Structural Topic Modelling. After computing the STM final Model, which resulted in 60 topics, we created a sub-dataset for our discourse analysis. Each topic included 50 representative tweets. For the sub-dataset, we collected the three tweets with the highest number of replies (the highest was n = 248 of replies, and the average replies were 16.15) from each of the 60 topics. This resulted in a dataset of 180 unique tweets.

sections.paper_id: Goodman et al. - 2024 - All Lives Matter discussions on Twitter Varied use, prevalence, and interpretive repertoires
paragraphs: For the computational analysis of our Twitter dataset, we used STM, a computational method to make meaning of big textual data. STM is a kind of topic modelling method that detects broad themes and patterns in textual data by calculating the co-occurrences of words, which then define topics. It is built on a topic modelling algorithm, Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), and is used to identify broad patterns of a large dataset by assigning documents (here tweets) to one or more topics. A topic (K) describes a group of words that has a 'semantically interpretable theme' (Roberts et al., 2014, p. 3). For example, when words like 'mouse' 'bird' and 'dog' co-occur in a dataset, they might get computed as one topic, which could be labelled 'urban animals'. STM is also a mixed membership model because it allows each document (here a tweet) to consist of a range of topics (Chung et al., 2022;Roberts et al., 2014). STM can 'account for polysemy (i.e., a word's different contextually based meanings)' (Chung et al., 2022, p. 6). An example is how the word 'mouse' could co-occur with other animals and words connected to technology (computer mouse). Thus, the same words can occur in different topics based on their different meanings. Finally, STM allows analyses of covariates like time or users to examine differences within topical prevalence and content. Based on our interest in the one-year timeline starting with the murder of Breonna Taylor, we added the metadata of time to examine the differences in topical content and prevalence in the dataset.

Our first run of STM resulted in a model of 83 topics. We ran this model with K = 0 to give us an initial impression of the data. To find the appropriate K for our model, we ran the searchK function with K = 40 to K = 90 in increments of 5 to determine the relative best fit of each model (Roberts et al., 2014;see Figure A1 (Diagnostic Ksearch). We then used the R package stminsights to decide the relative fit of the model. Both K = 55 and K = 60 seemed a good fit and we decided to use the STM model K = 60 (see Figure A2). We then used the findthoughts function in R to get the 50 most representative tweets of each of the 60 topics (n = 3000). Of these tweets, we selected the three tweets with the highest amount of replies (the highest was n = 248 of replies, and the average of replies were 16.15) from each of the 60 topics. This resulted in a dataset of 180 unique tweets.

STM was used as an entry point for the overarching analysis and as a sampling method for the following critical discursive psychological analysis. sections.text:
For the computational analysis of our Twitter dataset, we used STM, a computational method to make meaning of big textual data. STM is a kind of topic modelling method that detects broad themes and patterns in textual data by calculating the co-occurrences of words, which then define topics. It is built on a topic modelling algorithm, Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), and is used to identify broad patterns of a large dataset by assigning documents (here tweets) to one or more topics. A topic (K) describes a group of words that has a 'semantically interpretable theme' (Roberts et al., 2014, p. 3). For example, when words like 'mouse' 'bird' and 'dog' co-occur in a dataset, they might get computed as one topic, which could be labelled 'urban animals'. STM is also a mixed membership model because it allows each document (here a tweet) to consist of a range of topics (Chung et al., 2022;Roberts et al., 2014). STM can 'account for polysemy (i.e., a word's different contextually based meanings)' (Chung et al., 2022, p. 6). An example is how the word 'mouse' could co-occur with other animals and words connected to technology (computer mouse). Thus, the same words can occur in different topics based on their different meanings. Finally, STM allows analyses of covariates like time or users to examine differences within topical prevalence and content. Based on our interest in the one-year timeline starting with the murder of Breonna Taylor, we added the metadata of time to examine the differences in topical content and prevalence in the dataset.

Our first run of STM resulted in a model of 83 topics. We ran this model with K = 0 to give us an initial impression of the data. To find the appropriate K for our model, we ran the searchK function with K = 40 to K = 90 in increments of 5 to determine the relative best fit of each model (Roberts et al., 2014;see Figure A1 (Diagnostic Ksearch). We then used the R package stminsights to decide the relative fit of the model. Both K = 55 and K = 60 seemed a good fit and we decided to use the STM model K = 60 (see Figure A2). We then used the findthoughts function in R to get the 50 most representative tweets of each of the 60 topics (n = 3000). Of these tweets, we selected the three tweets with the highest amount of replies (the highest was n = 248 of replies, and the average of replies were 16.15) from each of the 60 topics. This resulted in a dataset of 180 unique tweets.

sections.paper_id: Goodman et al. - 2024 - All Lives Matter discussions on Twitter Varied use, prevalence, and interpretive repertoires
Goodman et al. - 2024 - All Lives Matter discussions on Twitter Varied use, prevalence, and interpretive repertoires
paragraphs: Table 1 shows, there was a diverse set of ALM conversations being expressed. Among the top ten conversations, half were critical of ALM. These include issues that critique ALM (T3, T17, T1) and the use of the hashtag ALM, as anti-racism messages (T7, T54), totalling in 18.9% of the 60 topic proportions. The other half of the top 10 topics reinforce ALM, with some conversations ascribing supposedly inclusive and liberal/positive/colourblind representations of ALM (T2, T11, T6, and T23), and others directly criticising Black Lives Matter (T41), totalling in 12.4% of the 60 topic proportions. The top 10 topics made up 31.3% of the 60 topics. Interestingly, the ALM hashtag as a right-wing marker (e.g., maga, womenfortrump, and trump) was not one of the most salient topics.

After coding the sub-dataset with 180 tweets, based on a deep reading, we found thirteen tweet groupings that describe how the ALM hashtag was used. Table 2 illustrates the diversity of its different uses ordered by the highest topic, along with one example tweet for each tweet grouping. Again, criticising ALM as an ideology/counternarrative and using the ALM hashtag to make an anti-racism argument were the two top topic conversations.

paragraphs: Figure 1 demonstrates that the #AllLivesMatter was used predominantly in the month of the killing of George Floyd which sparked the Summer 2020 Black Lives Matter protests.

Table 3 shows that the highest number of tweets were posted on June 3, 2020, nine days after George Floyd was murdered by the police on May 25, 2020. This table also shows that Twitter users used an average of 3 hashtags per tweet ranging from 2.39 hashtags per tweet to four.

To examine if there were differences between the three topics, we ran a time plot of Topics 3, 17, and 41. The first two topics' content included criticism of ALM, while T41 critiqued Black Lives Matter. With their different political orientations, we wanted to know whether there would be different distributions of tweets over time. Figure 2 shows almost identical time distributions of these three topics. The main differences lie between their expected topic proportions and a slight decrease of T17 at the end of the 364 days, contrasting T3 and T41's increase at the end of the graph.

sections.paper_id: Goodman et al. - 2024 - All Lives Matter discussions on Twitter Varied use, prevalence, and interpretive repertoires
paragraphs: As the previous analysis demonstrated, the STM resulted in 12 salient topics of ALM, with the top two topics comprising tweets criticising ALM and ALM being used as an anti-racism message. To this end, these topics were the primary focus of our critical discursive psychological analysis. In particular, we analysed what discursive repertoires were drawn upon, being used, and to what end, when criticising ALM and when using ALM for anti-racism. The next sections elaborate on these different uses of ALM. These six tweets all contain criticism of All Lives Matter by using the ALM hashtag. A common way that ALM is criticised is through references to racism, with some accusations of racism being more explicit than others. In this first example, the tweet consists of the repetition (eight times in total) of the explicit statement that ALM is racist.
Distinctly, the repetitive listing of #AllLivesMatter is racist is a frequently used pattern of language that demonstrates the comprehensiveness of an issue (Jefferson, 1990):

Tweet 1: Say it with me now. #AllLivesMatter is racist. #AllLivesMatter is racist. #AllLivesMatter is racist. #AllLivesMatter is racist. #AllLivesMatter is racist. #AllLivesMatter is racist. #AllLivesMatter is racist. #AllLivesMatter is racist. #AllLivesMatter is racist. (T3, 2020-05-27, retweet 73, replies 20, liked 309) Other examples are a little less explicit, but still clear, as seen in this example: Tweet 2: If you say #AllLivesMatter without saying #BlackLivesMatter then you don't really believe #AllLivesMatter If you deliberately say #AllLivesMatter instead of saying #BlackLivesMatter then you're probably a … racist. (T3, 2020-07-26, retweet 0, replies 6, liked 87)

This tweet contains an 'if x, then y' structure which infers what tweeters think, so that (x) if people say ALM without also saying BLM, then (y) they cannot really accept that all lives matter. This structure is then upgraded so that if this is done deliberately then (y) this infers racism. This accusation is made with a little hedging ('probably') and with the use of ellipses to present this racism as something that is being revealed. This next example includes a more subtle accusation of racism, again with the same 'if x, then y' structure to draw a contrast between Black and blue lives and again ending in a subtle accusation of racism, through implying that it is the word Black (e.g., Black people) that bothers ALM (and Blue Lives Matter) hashtag users (Dickerson, 2000).

T17, and T41 distributed over time. Tweet 3: 'If #BlackLivesMatter bothers you because it doesn't say #AllLivesMatter but #BlueLivesMatter doesn't bother you, then what really bothers you is the word "BLACK"'. (T51, 2020-09-24, retweet 17, replies 5, liked 40)

This next example does not include an implicit accusation of racism, but it does include an argument that ALM works to support a racist status quo. This is therefore an example of an attempt to recontextualise ALM by referring to the racist context in which BLM emerged (Goodman et al., 2023).

Tweet 4: If you, or anyone, is using or starts using the hashtag #AllLivesMatter … then put that hashtag back in its box. Until that actually becomes a truth, don't use it to excuse and defend ingrained racism in the United States for centuries. (T20, 2020-06-02, retweet 7, replies 5, liked 82)

These last two examples do not make references to racism or racist contexts but do attempt to undermine the logic of ALM and the morality of those that use it. In the following tweet, those that support ALM are presented as being the same as people who put the economy before people and therefore lacking in care for others. Here ALM users are insulted through the use of 'hoes', before the logic of ALM is criticised through the use of an analogy of a broken bone, where BLM is equated with wanting to treat the broken bone whereas ALM is equated with treating all bones. This works to argue that like treating all bones would be to miss the context, that ALM is also missing the context in which Black people face discrimination. It is not unusual to identify tweets that make this point (see Goodman et al., 2023 for a similar analogy) but what is noteworthy here is that the criticism of ALM is being brought about through the use of the ALM (rather than BLM) hashtag.

These last two examples do not make references to racism or racist contexts but do attempt to undermine the logic of ALM and the morality of those that use it. In the following tweet, those that support ALM are presented as being the same as people who put the economy before people and therefore lacking in care for others. Here ALM users are insulted through the use of 'hoes', before the logic of ALM is criticised through the use of an analogy of a broken bone, where BLM is equated with wanting to treat the broken bone whereas ALM is equated with treating all bones. This works to argue that like treating all bones would be to miss the context, that ALM is also missing the context in which Black people face discrimination. It is not unusual to identify tweets that make this point (see Goodman et al., 2023 for a similar analogy) but what is noteworthy here is that the criticism of ALM is being brought about through the use of the ALM (rather than BLM) hashtag.
sections.preview_head: As the previous analysis demonstrated, the STM resulted in 12 salient topics of ALM, with the top two topics comprising tweets criticising ALM and ALM being used as an anti-racism message. To this end, these topics were the primary focus of our critical discursive psychological analysis. In particular, we analysed what discursive repertoires were drawn upon, being used, and to what end, when criticising ALM and when using ALM for anti-racism.

sections.paper_id: Goodman et al. - 2024 - All Lives Matter discussions on Twitter Varied use, prevalence, and interpretive repertoires
sections.header_raw: | Support of BLM and anti-racism messages
sections.header_norm: support of blm and anti racism messages
paragraphs: In these examples, support for BLM and anti-racist messages continue to be displayed through the use of the ALM hashtag, although in these cases there is no accompanying explicit criticism of ALM. These next two tweets contain content that may be expected to be seen included alongside the BLM hashtag.

Tweet 7: Remember them… George Floyd Ahmoud Arbery Sandra Bland Sean Reed Tamir Rice Eric Garner Oscar Grant Michael Brown Walter Scott Freddie Gray Dante Parker Remember all of them. #MinneapolisPoliceMurderdHim #GeorgeLloyd #GeorgeFloydWasMurdered #AllLivesMatter. (T10, 2020-05-27, retweet 2175, replies 107, liked 6032)
The STM analysis of the data identified 60 salient topics that the Twitter public expressed via the ALM hashtag.

Some of these regnant conversations were expected and totalled 22.2% of topic conversations. Within these particular conversations, people used the ALM hashtag to find fault with BLM ('…violent looters, criminals, ppl burning good businesses. It's reprehensible'), to express a supposedly liberal inclusive colourblind version of the world (e.g., 'no one is left behind, and not because of the colour of the skin…life is a miracle that transcends color'), and as a marker of right-wing ideology ('Dems are a lawless party, not protecting us. #EndDemFacism'). Other conversations were novel, including using the hashtag to directly denounce ALM as racist ('Say it with me now.

#AllLivesMatter is racist'). In fact, half of the top conversations directly problematised ALM, totalling 18.9% of topic conversations. This suggests that Twitter users used various strategies, including using the ALM hashtag itself to have direct conversations about racism. In sum, we identified twelve thematic groupings, with the top ALM topics comprising criticisms of ALM. To understand important moments in time-related conversations, we also examined topic changes over time of three ALM hashtags, two that criticised ALM and one that criticised BLM. Notably, using the ALM hashtag to criticise ALM were the more predominant conversations over time in comparison to using the ALM hashtag as criticism of BLM. However, these discussions followed a similar pattern over time.

Putting a spotlight on the top two topics, our critical discursive psychological analysis showed that the way the Twitter public used the ALM hashtag to directly critique ALM was by directly naming ALM as being imbued with racism or a racist status quo. The particular repertoires used to accomplish this were via repertoires of repetition, recontextualising (as opposed to decontextualising) racism, and by placing ALM within a moral discourse. Discursively, directly naming racism has not been shown to be a common feature of race talk, particularly among members of majoritised groups (Augoustinos & Every, 2007;Goodman, 2014;Van Dijk, 1993). This could be because critical discourse analysis has primarily focused on 'top-down' relations of domination as opposed to discourses of resistance to racism (Van Dijk, 1993). Likewise, some recent research suggests that there may not be such a clear norm against purporting prejudice operating in online settings (Burke, Diba, & Antonopoulos, 2020;Goodman et al., 2023). The second way the Twitter public used the ALM hashtag was to support BLM and broader anti-racism messages. The particular repertoire used to accomplish this was through the language of pragmatism related to the harshness of police officers' encounters with Black people and therefore wanting critique ('…the brutal knee to the neck technique against civilians'). Here, Twitter users only subtly named ALM as racist, which is reflective of the careful discursive work around making direct accusations seen in previous discourse analytic studies on race talk.

Taken together, our study offers rich interpretations of large-scale conversations on Twitter and tells us important things about a segment of society's conversations around ALM. First, the STM showed that users did not exclusively use the ALM hashtag to advance a right-wing agenda. To be clear, these findings do not necessarily dispute previous research that has found a relationship between racism and support for the All Lives Matter (Paul, 2019;West et al., 2021). Nor do we think our data is indicative of a discursive shift in the discursive functioning of ALM; rather, we think this research suggests the ALM hashtag does not neatly map onto one particular dialogue happening on Twitter, but is instead, evoking diverse sets of conversations on Twitter, some contest, reproduce, also resist dominant discourses.

Likewise, a key feature of the results was critical linguistic doubling, namely how multiple and sometimes conflicting knowledges were being produced through the ALM hashtag. Twitter users moved back and forth between seemingly diverging evaluations, one that uses the ALM hashtag as a counternarrative to ALM and one that embraced ALM. Similar to Goodman et al.'s, analysis (2023), criticism of ALM was used where there was an acceptance that racism exists and ALM was embraced where racism was decontextualised through ideologies of colour-blindness. These results are also indicative of Molly Andrews' (Andrews et al., 2004) earlier work on counternarratives, where East Germans borrowed language of the revolutionaries, like 'power by the people' and 'we are the real Germans'. More broadly, these seemingly divergent discourses are also likely indicative of how racialised discourses have been paradoxically structured and reinforced through Western liberal ideologies of tolerance of difference and protection of the majoritised. Together, this suggests that the use of the ALM hashtag is not a settled debate provoking a need for further explication of thick meanings of language/language use beyond simple tropes and recognising that popular discourses have multiple conflicting currents with a non-fixed meaning.

In considering the results of this study, it is important to note some limitations, some of which present opportunities for future research. Hashtag only queries (e.g., scraping Twitter for the ALM hashtag) likely exclude tweets that are part of the ALM conversation, even though they did not use any hashtag. A second limitation is the focus on only English-language tweets. BLM prompted social uprisings in over fifty countries across the Global North and South, including Mexico, Colombia, Indonesia, and parts of Africa (Westerman et al., 2020). We likely missed important tweets because of our selection for English-language tweets only. Future research should cast a wider net examining multiple languages on Twitter and scrape the data for any tweets that include the term ALM. Finally, our critical discursive psychological analysis was limited in scope, focusing solely on the top two salient topics informed from the STM. Hence, the data have not been subject to the most detailed discourse analysis as the focus here has been on the models; however, a more thorough analysis of this data is likely to be further illuminating.

In closing, the hashtags #BlackLivesMatters and #AllLivesMatter have brought to the fore, yet again, public debates about racism and racialised violence. Our study showed the ways in which people are actively engaging on this issue, particularly through the hashtag, #AllLives Matter. Our data suggest that, at least on Twitter, the ALM hashtag is not one dimensional, nor is it a settled debate. Moreover, the Twitter public are using the ALM hashtag to denounce racism and the ideological pretex of AllLivesMatter. This holds great promise for the possibility of how publics talk, debate, and interact around issues of race and racism.
